perturbation.original_text: All Evaluation Material (i) shall be used solely for the purpose of evaluating and considering the Transaction; (ii) shall be kept strictly confidential by the Receiving Party; and (iii) shall be provided by the Receiving Party solely to those of its Representatives to whom disclosure is reasonably required to facilitate the Receiving Party's evaluation or consideration of the Transaction, it being the intention of the Parties to restrict the dissemination of Evaluation Material to as small a working group as practicable.
perturbation.changed_text: All Evaluation Material (i) should be used for the purpose of evaluating and considering the Transaction; (ii) shall be kept confidential by the Receiving Party; and (iii) shall be provided by the Receiving Party to its Representatives to whom disclosure seems appropriate to facilitate the Receiving Party's evaluation or consideration of the Transaction, it being the intention of the Parties to perhaps restrict the dissemination of Evaluation Material.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'shall be used solely' to 'should be used,' 'strictly confidential' to 'confidential,' 'reasonably required' to 'seems appropriate,' and introducing 'perhaps restrict,' the obligation becomes ambiguous and discretionary. This weakens the confidentiality and use restrictions, potentially allowing for broader use and dissemination of the Evaluation Material.
perturbation.law_explanation: The GTSA protects trade secrets, requiring reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. By weakening the language regarding confidentiality and limiting dissemination, the modified text undermines the 'reasonable efforts' standard required for trade secret protection under O.C.G.A. § 10-1-741. The original text clearly mandates strict confidentiality and restricted use, whereas the modified text introduces uncertainty and discretion, potentially failing to meet the legal threshold for protecting trade secrets in Georgia.
perturbation.location: 3. Confidentiality and Use of Evaluation Material. (a)

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity that undermines the original intention to maintain strict confidentiality and limited dissemination of evaluation material, potentially failing to meet the 'reasonable efforts' standard required for trade secret protection. The change from 'shall be used solely' to 'should be used,' 'strictly confidential' to 'confidential,' 'reasonably required' to 'seems appropriate,' and introducing 'perhaps restrict' weakens the legal obligation. Scraped Law Snippet 2, although incomplete, hints at the importance of terms and conditions, suggesting that any ambiguity introduced could lead to disputes and impact the enforceability of the contract, supporting the existence of a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party shall be responsible for any breach of this Agreement by any of its Representatives.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party is generally responsible for breaches of this Agreement by its Representatives.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall be responsible' to 'is generally responsible' introduces ambiguity about the extent of liability. It suggests that responsibility is not absolute, which creates uncertainty around enforcement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General principles of agency law under Georgia law.
perturbation.law_explanation: O.C.G.A. § 10-6-51 outlines the principal's (in this case, each Party) responsibility for the actions of their agents (Representatives). The modified text uses 'generally responsible' which implies that there are exceptions to the liability, contradicting the common law principle that a principal is liable for the acts of their agents within the scope of their authority.
perturbation.location: 3. Confidentiality and Use of Evaluation Material. (a)

perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces ambiguity regarding the principal's liability for the acts of their agents. The original text states the party 'shall be responsible,' imposing a clear obligation. The changed text weakens this to 'is generally responsible,' implying exceptions to liability. This directly contradicts O.C.G.A. § 10-6-51, as evidenced by Scraped Law Snippet 1, which states, "The principal shall be bound by all the acts of his agent within the scope of his authority." The modified text introduces ambiguity about the extent of the principal's liability, which undermines the clarity and enforceability of the original clause, creating a contradiction. Scraped Law Snippet 2 is not valid and cannot be used.

perturbation.original_text: If the Receiving Party or its Representatives are requested or required in any judicial, arbital or administrative proceeding or by any governmental or regulatory authority to disclose any Evaluation Material (whether by deposition, interrogatory, request for documents, subpoena, civil investigative demand, or otherwise), or the Receiving Party is so requested or required to disclose any of the facts disclosure of which is prohibited under paragraph (3)(c) of this Agreement, the Receiving Party shall give the Furnishing Party prompt notice of such request so that the Furnishing Party may seek an appropriate protective order or other appropriate remedy and/or waive compliance with the provisions of this Agreement, and, upon the Furnishing Party's request and at the Furnishing Party's expense, shall reasonably cooperate with the Furnishing Party in seeking such an order.
perturbation.changed_text: If the Receiving Party or its Representatives are requested in any judicial, arbital or administrative proceeding or by any governmental or regulatory authority to disclose any Evaluation Material, the Receiving Party will endeavor to give the Furnishing Party notice of such request so that the Furnishing Party may seek a protective order. The Receiving Party will attempt to cooperate with the Furnishing Party in seeking such an order.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'are requested or required...shall give prompt notice' with 'are requested...will endeavor to give notice' weakens the mandatory obligation. Also, 'shall reasonably cooperate' has been replaced with 'will attempt to cooperate'. This introduces uncertainty about the Receiving Party's obligation to notify the Furnishing Party and cooperate with them.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Georgia Rules of Civil Procedure concerning discovery and protective orders.
perturbation.law_explanation: O.C.G.A. § 9-11-26(c) allows parties to seek protective orders to protect confidential information from disclosure in legal proceedings. By changing the mandatory 'shall' to the weaker 'will endeavor' and 'will attempt,' the contract weakens the Receiving Party's commitment to assist the Furnishing Party in seeking a protective order, potentially hindering the latter's ability to protect its confidential information as intended by the law.
perturbation.location: 3. Confidentiality and Use of Evaluation Material. (b)
perturbation.justification: The modification weakens the mandatory obligation of the Receiving Party to notify and cooperate with the Furnishing Party in seeking a protective order. The original text uses 'shall give prompt notice' and 'shall reasonably cooperate,' which are strong mandates. The changed text replaces these with 'will endeavor to give notice' and 'will attempt to cooperate,' introducing uncertainty and making the obligations discretionary. While no law snippets were provided, the Law Explanation states that O.C.G.A. § 9-11-26(c) allows parties to seek protective orders, and the weakened language in the modified contract could hinder the Furnishing Party's ability to protect its confidential information as intended by the law, making it more difficult to enforce the obligation to assist in obtaining a protective order. This aligns with the definition of 'Ambiguities - Ambiguous Legal Obligation' and constitutes a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: For a period of one year subsequent to the termination of discussions regarding a Transaction, neither Party shall, without the prior written consent of the other Party, directly or indirectly solicit for hire any person currently employed by the other Party (or any of its subsidiaries) with whom the hiring Party first has contact, or who first becomes known to the hiring Party, in the course of the Parties' discussions and due diligence with respect to the proposed Transaction; provided, however, that the foregoing provision shall not prevent either Party, without such consent, from employing any employee who (i) contacts the hiring Party directly at his or her own initiative without any direct or indirect solicitation by or encouragement from the hiring Party, (ii) responds to a mass media solicitation or advertisement consistent with the hiring Party's past practices that is not directed at employees of the other Party or (iii) is identified by a third party executive search firm or employment agency without any assistance from such Party.
perturbation.changed_text: For roughly a year after the termination of discussions, neither Party should, without consent, solicit for hire employees of the other Party with whom the hiring Party has contact during discussions; provided that this doesn't prevent hiring employees who contact the hiring Party or respond to general advertisements.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'one year' with 'roughly a year' introduces ambiguity about the duration of the non-solicitation agreement. Replacing 'shall' with 'should' weakens the prohibition on solicitation. Omitting 'directly or indirectly' and shortening the rest of the provisions further weakens the scope of the provision.
perturbation.law_explanation: Under O.C.G.A. § 13-8-53, restrictive covenants, including non-solicitation agreements, must be reasonable in time, territory, and scope of activity. The weakened language regarding the duration ('roughly a year') and the obligation ('should') creates uncertainty. The removal of 'directly or indirectly' reduces the restrictions that are in place. This could render the covenant unenforceable because the Georgia Restrictive Covenants Act requires clarity and precision in the terms of the restriction. The Georgia Supreme Court has stressed the importance of precise language in restrictive covenants.

Enforcement of Covenants; Writing Requirement; Determining Competitive Status; Effect of Failure to Comply; Time and Geographic Limitations Universal Citation: GA Code § 13-8-53 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next Notwithstanding any other provision of this chapter, enforcement of contracts that restrict competition during the term of a restrictive covenant, so long as such restrictions are reasonable in time, geographic area, and scope of prohibited activities, shall be permitted. However, enforcement of contracts that restrict competition after the term of employment, as distinguished from a customer nonsolicitation provision, as described in subsection (b) of this Code section, or a nondisclosure of confidential information provision, as described in subsection (e) of this Code section, shall not be permitted against any employee who does not, in the course of his or her employment: Customarily and regularly solicit for the employer customers or prospective customers; Customarily and regularly engage in making sales or obtaining orders or contracts for products or services to be performed by others; Perform the following duties: Have a primary duty of managing the enterprise in which the employee is employed or of a customarily recognized department or subdivision thereof; Customarily and regularly direct t
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding the non-solicitation agreement's duration by changing 'one year' to 'roughly a year,' and weakens the prohibition on solicitation by replacing 'shall' with 'should.' Additionally, the omission of 'directly or indirectly' diminishes the scope of the provision. According to the provided Law Explanation, O.C.G.A. § 13-8-53 requires restrictive covenants to be reasonable in time and scope. The scraped law snippet, Georgia Code § 13-8-53 (2022), supports the enforcement of contracts that restrict competition if the restrictions are reasonable in time, geographic area, and scope of prohibited activities. The altered language creates uncertainty and reduces restrictions, potentially rendering the covenant unenforceable under Georgia law, as clarity and precision are crucial.

perturbation.original_text: At any time after termination of discussions by either Party with respect to the Transaction, upon the request of the Furnishing Party, the Receiving Party shall promptly (and in no event later than five business days after such request) (a) redeliver or cause to be redelivered to the Furnishing Party all copies of all Primary Evaluation Material in the possession or control of the Receiving Party or its Representatives which is in a visual or written format and erase or destroy all copies of all such Primary Evaluation Material which is stored in electronic format, and (b) destroy or cause to be destroyed all Derivative Evaluation Material in the possession or control of the Receiving Party or any of its Representatives.
perturbation.changed_text: After termination of discussions, the Receiving Party should, upon request, endeavor to return Primary Evaluation Material and destroy Derivative Evaluation Material.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text replaces the specific deadline ('shall promptly (and in no event later than five business days after such request)') with a vague obligation ('should endeavor'). This weakens the obligation to return or destroy the materials within a defined timeframe.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General contract law principles regarding reasonable time and performance in Georgia.
perturbation.law_explanation: O.C.G.A. § 13-4-20 requires contracts to be performed within a reasonable time when no specific time is provided. The original clause provides a specific timeframe. The modified clause replaces 'shall promptly' and the specific 5-day timeframe with 'should endeavor,' making the time for performance indefinite and potentially unreasonable under Georgia contract law. This ambiguity could lead to disputes over whether the Receiving Party fulfilled its obligation.
perturbation.location: 6. Return and Destruction of Evaluation Material.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Georgia Code § 13-4-20 (2022) - Requirements as to Performance of Contractual Obligations Generally :: 2022 Georgia Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Georgia Code 2023 Georgia Code 2022 Georgia Code (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Georgia Code Title 13 - Contracts Chapter 4 - Modification, Extinguishment, and Renewal Article 2 - Performance § 13-4-20. Requirements as to Performance of Contractual Obligations Generally Universal Citation: GA Code § 13-4-20 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Next Performance, to be effectual, must be accomplished by the party bound to perform, or by his agent where personal skill is not required, or by someone substituted, by consent, in his place, and must be substantially in compliance with the spirit and the letter of the contract and completed within a reasonable time. History. Orig. Code 1863, § 2811; Code 1868, § 2819; Code 1873, § 2870; Code 1882, § 2870; Civil Code 1895, § 3724; Civil Code 1910, § 4318; Code 1933, § 20-1101. Cross references. Delegation of performance under Uniform Commercial Code, § 11-2-210 . Effect of absence of specific time provision in sales contracts under Uniform Commercial Code, § 11-2-309 . Duty of administrator or executor to fulfill, when possible, decedent’s contractual obligations, § 53-7-9. Law reviews. For article discussing failure of consideration, see 4 Mercer L. Rev. 327 (1953). For article surveying recent legislative and judicial developments in Georgia’s real property laws, see 31 Mercer L. Rev. 187 (1979). perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation by replacing a specific deadline with a vague one. The original text states 'shall promptly (and in no event later than five business days after such request)', while the changed text says 'should endeavor.' This contradicts Georgia Code § 13-4-20 (2022), which states performance must be 'completed within a reasonable time.' By removing the five-day deadline and replacing it with 'should endeavor,' the modified text introduces ambiguity about what constitutes a 'reasonable time,' creating a potential contradiction. The original text aligns with the requirement of a defined timeframe, while the changed text undermines it. Scraped Law Snippet 2 is not helpful.
